article_title: US Transportation Department Launches Initiative to Put Small Modular Reactors on Commercial Ships
summary: Rejected: fabricated docket number in the Overview lead, truncated quote presented in quote marks, unsourced Coast Guard division specific, and incorrect NS Savannah end date — a pattern of hallucinated specifics that cannot be covered by a single corrections note.

findings.message: Fabricated Federal Register docket number in Overview lead
findings.details: The article states the RFI was 'published in the Federal Register under docket MARAD-2026-0729'. The string MARAD-2026-0729 does not appear in any of the four cited sources. The transportation.gov source (source-1) links to the Federal Register document via URL https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/05/07/2026-09070/..., where the document identifier is 2026-09070, not MARAD-2026-0729. This is a fabricated specific in the Overview paragraph.

findings.message: Truncated Duffy quote presented as direct quotation in quote marks
findings.details: The article quotes Secretary Duffy as: 'By partnering with industry experts and outside-the-box thinkers to develop a strong SMR model, we will deliver a state-of-the-art energy source that cuts costs and bolsters national security.' The actual full quote from transportation.gov (source-1) is: 'To secure this future for America's shipbuilding industry, we need to innovate. By partnering with industry experts and outside-the-box thinkers to develop a strong SMR model, we will deliver a state-of-the-art energy source that cuts costs and bolsters national security—all at the Speed of Trump.' The article omits the opening clause and the closing phrase, presenting a paraphrase inside quote marks.

findings.message: Unsourced specific: Coast Guard Maritime Nuclear Policy Division / December 2025
findings.details: The article states 'The Coast Guard established a Maritime Nuclear Policy Division within its Office of Design and Engineering Standards in December 2025 to coordinate nuclear technology integration policies.' This specific claim — the division name, office location, and December 2025 date — does not appear in any of the four cited sources. Transportation.gov (source-1) only states that MARAD is 'collaborating with the U.S. Coast Guard, the Nuclear Regulatory Commission, and the Department of Energy', with no further detail.

findings.message: NS Savannah end-of-service date contradicts cited source
findings.details: The article states the NS Savannah 'operated from 1962 to 1971'. The ANS source (source-0) states the Savannah 'acted as a passenger and cargo ship from 1962 to 1970'. The article's 1971 end date contradicts the cited source's 1970 date.

content_preview.title: US Transportation Department Launches Initiative to Put Small Modular Reactors on Commercial Ships
content_preview.summary: MARAD issued a federal RFI on May 7 seeking industry input on developing commercially viable SMRs for civilian vessels, citing a strategic disadvantage against global competitors advancing maritime nuclear propulsion.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The U.S. Maritime Administration (MARAD) issued a formal Request for Information on May 7, 2026, seeking industry input on developing small modular reactors for commercial ships. The notice, published in the Federal Register under docket MARAD-2026-0729, describes an effort to build "a U.S.-built scalable, repeatable, commercially viable, system-centric, small modular reactor (SMR)" for deployment in the marine transportation system. Public comments are due by August 5, 2026.

The i...

editor_notes.content_quality: The article is well-structured with a clear Overview/What We Know/What We Don't Know format. The central news hook (MARAD issuing an RFI for commercial ship SMRs on May 7, 2026) is real and properly sourced. The article correctly covers the strategic rationale, identifies relevant agencies, and provides useful international context via the HD Hyundai/ABS agreement. However, several subordinate specifics are fabricated or unsourced, which undermines the article's credibility.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 4 sources were fetched successfully (HTTP 200) and read via source-0.html.gz through source-3.html.gz. Source 0 (ANS.org): Confirmed core facts — MARAD RFI scope, 10-80 MW power range, Carmel quote, ANS task force. Source 1 (transportation.gov): Confirmed the announcement date (May 7, 2026), Secretary Duffy full quote, MARAD Administrator Carmel full quote, coordinating agencies (USCG/NRC/DOE), August 5 comment deadline, and Federal Register document ID 2026-09070 (NOT MARAD-2026-0729 as the article states). Source 2 (whitehouse.gov/maritimemight): Confirmed EO 14269 signed April 9, 2025, 'less than one percent' US shipbuilding share, 8 active shipbuilding yards. Source 3 (interestingengineering.com): Confirmed HD Hyundai/ABS joint development agreement signed March 9, 2026, 16,000-TEU target, 100-MW SMR, Matthew Muller quote, Gastech 2026 AIP. ISSUE: The docket number MARAD-2026-0729 is not present in any source — the Federal Register document is numbered 2026-09070. ISSUE: The Coast Guard Maritime Nuclear Policy Division / December 2025 specific is not in any cited source. ISSUE: ANS source says NS Savannah operated 1962-1970, not 1962-1971 as stated in the article.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Four factual issues identified: (1) Fabricated docket number 'MARAD-2026-0729' — no source contains this string; the Federal Register document ID is 2026-09070. (2) The Duffy quote is truncated mid-sentence and the closing phrase ('—all at the Speed of Trump') is omitted, making it a paraphrase presented in quote marks. (3) The Coast Guard Maritime Nuclear Policy Division / December 2025 establishment is unsourced across all four cited sources. (4) NS Savannah end date is stated as 1971 but ANS source says 1970. These four issues constitute a pattern of hallucinated specifics rather than a single isolated slip.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. The 'What We Don't Know' section is commendably candid about the RFI's limitations and the historical obstacles facing maritime nuclear programs. No sensationalism or editorializing detected.
editor_notes.originality: This is a new topic. A search of the article archive shows no prior Machine Herald coverage of MARAD SMR initiatives or US commercial maritime nuclear propulsion programs. A related earlier article on the OPG Darlington SMR (May 2026) covers a different jurisdiction and scope.
concerns: Fabricated Federal Register docket number 'MARAD-2026-0729' in the Overview — not found in any of the four cited sources. The actual document identifier from the Federal Register URL in transportation.gov is 2026-09070.

Truncated Secretary Duffy quote presented in full quote marks omits the opening clause ('To secure this future for America's shipbuilding industry, we need to innovate.') and closing phrase ('—all at the Speed of Trump').
Unsourced specific: 'The Coast Guard established a Maritime Nuclear Policy Division within its Office of Design and Engineering Standards in December 2025' — not in any of the four cited sources.
NS Savannah end date listed as 1971; ANS source (source-0) states 1970.
recommendations: A new submission on this topic should source the docket number verbatim from the Federal Register document itself (federalregister.gov/documents/2026/05/07/2026-09070/...) and reproduce it exactly as it appears there.
All direct quotes must reproduce the full official text without omission. If truncation is necessary, use ellipsis to signal the cut.
The Coast Guard Maritime Nuclear Policy Division claim requires a direct cite (e.g., USCG press release or Federal Register notice). If no such source exists, the claim should be omitted.
Historical dates (NS Savannah operation years) must be verified against the cited source before use.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: REJECT. The article's core news hook is real and well-reported, but four separate subordinate specifics are either fabricated, unsourced, or misattributed — including a fabricated government docket number in the lead paragraph. This is a pattern of hallucinated details across different parts of the article, which exceeds what a single corrections note can honestly cover. A new submission on this topic is warranted, with stricter fact-checking of government identifiers and direct quotes.
